title: Component Memory Management

Component Memory Management

Component Memory Management

At runtime, component instances require memory to handle the component definition and component data. This memory is not necessarily freed when the component instance is deleted.

Memory that is used to initially execute a component can be reused by subsequent executions of the same component, depending on the component's Component Behavior property.

Note: Reusing component memory is intended and best suited to Form components that are started with the run command. Such forms are common in older Uniface applications. They are modal, and often access large amounts of data.

Reusing component memory is less suited to applications that are developed using the principles and features of component-based processing (purpose-specific component types, the ability to create multiple component instances using newinstance, and being able to activate operations on those component instances). Moreover, it violates the central principle of component-based development, which is that a component instance and its data from a modular, cohesive unit.

Component Definitions

The first time a component instance is created with the newinstance, activate, or run ProcScript statements, the definition data for the component is loaded from the file system into memory. The size of a component definition in memory is about the same as the uncompressed size of the component definition file (.dsp, .svc, and so on).

If the component is instantiated a second time with the same instance name (or run with the same form name), it uses the component definition already stored in memory, reducing the start-up time. To identify a component definition in memory, Uniface uses the instance name from the newinstance command (or the form name, for forms created with the run statement).

If you explicitly specify the component instance name, deleting a component instance does not necessarily unload the component definition. Unless you intend to reuse the component definitions, it is recommended that you set the component property Component Behavior to Drop component definition from memory. If you set it to another value, the component definition remains in memory, causing a memory leak.

Component Data

The amount of memory allocated for component data depends on the amount of data fetched from a database or loaded from a file, including component-, entity-, and field-level properties.

Component data in memory is available only within the scope of the component instance and is maintained by the component instance.

For components started with the run command, it is possible to keep the data in memory, even if the component has exited. To do so, set Component Behavior to Keep component definition and data in memory.

Note: There is no guarantee that the available component data will be synchronized with the database or any other data source.
Related Links
  • ProcScript for Managing Components

    Component-based development enables you to create multi-tier applications. There are various ProcScript instructions and functions for handling component-based processing.

  • Component Behavior

    Specifies whether the component definition is cached in memory.

  • activate

    Calls an operation on a component instance.

  • newinstance

    Creates a new instance of a component.
